concepts.title: Work Done By a Constant Force
concepts.conceptBody:
Work

It refers to an activity involving a force and movement in the direction of the force.

Work done by a constant force 

When the point at which a force acts moves, the force is said to have done work.

When the force is constant, the work done is defined as the product of the force and distance moved.

Work done $=$ force distance moved in direction of force 

Consider in Figure a force $\text{F}$ acting at the angle $\theta $ moves a body from point $\text{A}$ to point $\text{B}$.

The distance moved in the direction of the force is given by

Distance in direction of force $=\text{scos}\theta $

So the work done by the force $\text{F}$ is

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Work }\!\!~\!\!\text{ done }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\mathbf{F}\text{s cos}\theta $

If the body moves in the same direction as the force the angle is 0.0 so

\[\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Work }\!\!~\!\!\text{ done }\!\!~\!\!\text{ = }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Fs }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\]

When the angle is 90 then the work done is zero.

The SI units for work are Joules J (with force F, in Newton's N and distance, s, in metres $\text{m}$).

Note: Under the action of a constant force work done is path independent, i.e., it depends on initial and final positions only. For example, if an object takes from position A to position B through three different paths 1, 2 and 3 under a constant force say $\text{\vec{F}}=2\overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,+3\text{\vec{j}}+\overset{}{\mathop{\text{k}}}\,$, then work done by this force in all three paths will be same, or

${{\text{W}}_{1}}={{\text{W}}_{2}}={{\text{W}}_{3}}$

Similarly, work done by force of gravity $\left( \text{m \vec{g}} \right)$ near the surface of earth is path independent as force of gravity is constant. Although we will see later that being a conservative force work done by force of gravity is path independent at greater heights also.

concepts.title: Work Done By a Variable Force
concepts.conceptBody:
Work Done By a Variable Force

Forces in practice will often vary. Consider the case where the force varies as in Figure.

For the thin strip with width ds - shown shaded in Figure the force can be considered constant at $\text{F}$. The work done over the distance $\text{ds}$ is then

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ work }\!\!~\!\!\text{ done }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\text{F }\!\!~\!\!\text{ ds }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

This is the area of the shaded strip.

The total work done for distance $\text{s}$ is the sum of the areas of all such strips. This is the same as the area under the Force-distance.

Work done $=$ area under force/distance curve 

Clearly, this also works for a constant force the curve is then a horizontal line.

concepts.title: Formula for work done
concepts.conceptBody:
General Concept 

 

If point of application of force suffers an infinitesimal vector $\text{d} \vec{r}$ relative to a frame of reference $\text{S}$, then infinitesimal work done by force during infinitesimal displacement, relative to same frame of reference S.

$\text{dW}=\vec{F}\cdot \text{d}\vec{r}$

Two important points to be noted,

(i) frame of reference must be mentioned

(ii) point of application is important in case of rigid body.

$\text{W}=\int ~\text{dW}=\int ~\vec{F}\cdot \text{d} \vec{r}$

If $\vec{F}=$ constant

$\Delta\text{ W}=\vec{F}\cdot \Delta\vec{r}\left. \text{ [F }\text{ is }\text{ constant }\text{ in }\text{ magnitudes }\text{ as }\text{ well }\text{ as }\text{ in }\text{ direction } \right]$

concepts.title: Work done by spring force
concepts.conceptBody:
Work done by Spring Force

Consider the situation shown in figure. On end of a spring is attached to a fixed vertical support and the other end to a block which can move on a horizontal table. Let $\text{x}=$ denote the position of the block when the spring is in its natural length. When the block is displaced by an amount $\text{x}$ (either compressed or elongated) a restoring force $\left( \text{F} \right)$ is applied by the spring on the block. The direction of this force $\text{F}$ is always towards its mean position $\left( \text{x}=0 \right)$ and the magnitude is directly proportional to $\text{x}$ or 

$\text{F}\propto \text{x}$ (Hooke’s law)

$\text{F}=-\text{kx}$ …(i)

Here, $\text{k}$ is a constant called force constant of spring and depends on the nature of spring. From Eq. (i) we see that $\text{F}$ is variable force and $\text{F}$-x graph is a straight line passing through origin with slope $=-\text{k}$. Negative sign in Eq. (i) implies that the spring force $\text{F}$ is directed in a direction opposite to the displacement $\text{x}$ of the block

Let us now find the work done by this force $\text{F}$ when the block is displaced from $\text{x}=0$ to $\text{x}=\text{x}$. This can be obtained either by integration or the area under F-x graph.

Thus,

$\text{W}=\int \text{dW}=\int _{0}^{\text{x}}\text{Fdx}=\int _{0}^{\text{x}}\text{Fdx}=\int _{0}^{\text{x}}-\text{kxdx}=-\frac{1}{2}\text{k}{{\text{x}}^{2}}$

Here, work done is negative because force is in opposite direction of displacement.

Similarly, if the block moves from $\text{x}={{\text{x}}_{1}}$ to to $\text{x}={{\text{x}}_{2}}$. The limits of integration are ${{\text{x}}_{1}}$ and ${{\text{x}}_{2}}$ and the work done is

$\text{W}=\mathop{\int }_{{{\text{x}}_{1}}}^{{{\text{x}}_{2}}}-\text{kxdx}=\frac{1}{2}\text{k}\left( \text{x}_{1}^{2}-\text{x}_{2}^{2} \right)$

concepts.title: Work done relative to frame of reference
concepts.conceptBody:
Work done relative to frame of reference

The work done by friction on system of 2 bodies relative to all frame of reference will be equal, and

(i) will be negative if there is relative slipping between 2 bodies.

(ii) will be zero, if there is no relative slipping.

$\begin{align} & {{\text{W}}_{\text{fr}}}={{\overrightarrow{\text{f}}}_{\text{k}}}\cdot \left( \text{d}\overrightarrow{{{\text{r}}^{\prime }}}+\text{d}\overrightarrow{\text{r}} \right)-{{\text{f}}_{\text{k}}}\cdot (\text{d}\overrightarrow{\text{r}}) \\ & =\left( \overrightarrow{{{\text{f}}_{\text{k}}}}\cdot \text{d}{{{\vec{r}}}^{'}} \right)\text{ (negetive }\text{ in }\text{ sign)} \\ \end{align}$

$m v^2$
A body of mass $m$ is moving in a circle of radius $r$ with a constant speed $v$. The force on the body is $\frac{m v^2}{r}$ and is directed towards the centre. What is the work done by this force in moving the body over half the circumference of the circle
